question: How do students join the Office Hours or live workshop if the Zoom link isn't shared publicly?
answer_llm:
Students join via **YouTube Live**, not the Zoom link.

The Zoom link is only shared with **instructors/presenters/TAs**. For students, the live session video URL is posted in the **announcements channel on Telegram and Slack** before it starts, and the stream is also available on the **DataTalksClub YouTube channel**. Questions should be submitted through **Slido** when the session is live.

You can also watch live on the DataTalksClub [YouTube Channel](https://www.youtube.com/c/DataTalksClub).

Don’t post questions in chat as they may be missed if the room is very active.

question: What’s the best way to begin the LLM Zoomcamp if I’m joining late and want to know what to read first?
answer_llm:
Start with the **LLM Zoomcamp docs**, the **general Zoomcamp logistics docs**, and the **LLM Zoomcamp GitHub repository**.

If you’re joining late, you can still start anytime: the videos and GitHub materials are available, and the deadlines are on the **course management platform**.

A good order is:

1. Watch the lesson videos.
2. Work through the lesson notebooks/code.
3. Read the homework instructions on GitHub.
4. Submit answers on the course platform before the deadline.

For **Module 1**, begin here:
- [Module 1 cohort materials](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/tree/main/cohorts/2026/01-agentic-rag)
- [Module 1 homework instructions](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/cohorts/2026/01-agentic-rag/homework.md)

If something is unclear, first read the **GitHub homework instructions**, not only the course platform page. If it’s still unclear, ask in Slack and include code or command output as text, not a screenshot.

Start with the [LLM Zoomcamp docs](https://datatalks.club/docs/courses/llm-zoomcamp/), the [general Zoomcamp logistics docs](https://datatalks.club/docs/courses/zoomcamp-logistics/), and the [LLM Zoomcamp GitHub repository](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp).

question: I just joined and can’t find my name on the leaderboard — how do I figure out which row is mine?
answer_llm:
Click the **“Jump to your record on the leaderboard”** link — your account is automatically assigned a random display name, so that link will take you to your row.

If you want to see or change the name shown publicly, go to **“Edit Course Profile.”**
answer_orig:
When you set up your account, you are automatically assigned a random name, such as “Lucid Elbakyan.” Click on the "Jump to your record on the leaderboard" link to find your entry.

If you want to see what your Display name is, click on the "Edit Course Profile" button.

<{IMAGE:image_1}>

- **First field:** This is your nickname/displayed name. You can change it if you want to be known by your Slack username, GitHub username, or any other nickname of your choice. This is useful if you want to remain anonymous.
- **Second field:** Change this to your official name as in your identification documents—passport, national ID card, driver's license, etc. This is mandatory if you do not want "Lucid Elbakyan" on your certificate. This name will appear on your Certificate!

question: Why does the leaderboard show a random name for me instead of my real name, and can I change it?
answer_llm:
The leaderboard uses a separate public display name, which is assigned a random nickname by default, not your certificate name.

Yes, you can change it:
- Go to **Edit Course Profile**
- **First field**: your public nickname/display name for the leaderboard
- **Second field**: your official name for the certificate

So if you want, you can replace the random name with a nickname of your choice.

question: Why does a homework assignment keep changing before it's released?
answer_llm:
If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means the homework is not ready yet.

The homework is only ready when both are true:

- The homework form is open on the course management platform.
- The homework title does not contain `[DRAFT]`.

Until then, the content can still change. Working on it before release is at your own risk, because the final version may be different.

question: Why don't we use LangChain in this course, and what are we supposed to learn instead?
answer_llm:
Because LangChain is a framework for building LLM apps, and in this course we want you to first build the core pieces yourself: prompting, retrieval, indexing, and evaluation.

In other words, you’re supposed to learn the fundamentals before relying on a framework. The course compares this to learning HTML, CSS, and JavaScript before using React or Angular.
answer_orig:
LangChain is a framework for building LLM-powered apps. In this course, we first build the core pieces ourselves: prompting, retrieval, indexing, and evaluation.

Think of it like learning HTML, CSS, and JavaScript before using React or Angular. Frameworks are easier to use well once you understand what they automate.

question: Is LangChain required for the RAG module, or do we build the pipeline manually first?

question: Why do I get an OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits when I run responses.create?
answer_llm:
You’re getting that error because your OpenAI API account doesn’t have enough quota/credits to run `responses.create`.

In this course, OpenAI API usage is not free, so you need to:

1. Add credits to your OpenAI account
2. Make sure you’re using a model available to your account
3. If needed, create a new API key after adding credits

If you’re using OpenRouter instead of OpenAI, the fix is different: set a reasonable `max_output_tokens` value (for example, `1024`) and ensure your OpenRouter key has enough credits.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: How can I fix the error from OpenAI responses.create saying I don't have enough credits?
answer_llm:
If you’re getting an OpenAI `responses.create` error about **insufficient credits**, the fix depends on which service you’re actually using:

### If you’re using OpenAI directly
You need to **add billing credits** to your OpenAI account:
1. Go to your OpenAI billing page.
2. Add funds / enable pay-as-you-go.
3. Recreate your API key if needed.
4. Make sure you’re using a model available to your account.

### If you’re using OpenRouter
The issue is often that `responses.create()` is being called **without a low enough `max_output_tokens`**, so OpenRouter rejects the request before running the model.

Use a smaller limit, for example:

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

If you’re using `rag_helper.py`, add the same parameter in the `llm()` method.  
For ToyAIKit:

```python
llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

Also check that your `.env` points to OpenRouter and uses a pinned model, for example:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

If you want, I can help you determine whether your error is from **OpenAI** or **OpenRouter**.

question: Why am I getting a 429 RateLimitError from OpenAI, and does it always mean I've hit a rate limit?
answer_llm:
A 429 `RateLimitError` can mean two different things:

- **You actually hit a rate limit**: common on free-tier or low-spend accounts if you make too many requests per minute or per day.
- **You have a quota/billing problem**: if the error says `insufficient_quota`, it’s not a rate limit — it means your OpenAI API account has no available billing quota.

So, **no, a 429 does not always mean you hit a rate limit**.

If you’re seeing this during the course, the most common fixes are:
- reduce the number of API calls,
- lower concurrency,
- cache results,
- batch requests,
- and use automatic retries with backoff.

If the message is `insufficient_quota`, you need to add credits or fix billing first.
answer_orig:
```json
RateLimitError: Error code: 429 - {'error': {'message': 'You exceeded your current quota, please check your plan and billing details. For more information on this error, read the docs: [https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/error-codes/api-errors.](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/error-codes/api-errors.)', 'type': 'insufficient_quota', 'param': None, 'code': 'insufficient_quota'}
```

The above errors are related to your OpenAI API account’s quota. question: Why does the course recommend using uv instead of just sticking with pip and virtualenv?
answer_llm:
The course recommends `uv` because it’s faster and more convenient than managing separate tools like `pip` and `virtualenv`.

`uv` is an all-in-one tool that can replace several traditional Python tools, including:

- `pip`
- `virtualenv`
- `pip-tools`
- `pipx`
- `poetry`
- `pyenv`
- `twine`

So instead of juggling multiple commands and tools, you can manage Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies with one fast tool and reproducible lockfiles.

You do **not** have to use it though — `pip` and `virtualenv` still work fine. The course uses `uv` mainly because it makes setup simpler and faster.
answer_orig:

It's an all-in-one tool that replaces pip, virtualenv, pip-tools, pipx, poetry, pyenv, and twine — instead of juggling several tools, you manage Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies through one fast command with reproducible lockfiles.

You're not required to use it — pip and virtualenv still work fine — but uv makes the setup simpler and faster, which is why the lessons use it.

Official docs: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/

question: How do I keep my OpenAI or Groq API keys out of the code and load them from a .env file instead?
answer_llm:
Store the keys in a `.env` file and load them in Python with `python-dotenv`.

### 1) Add `.env` to `.gitignore`
```gitignore
.env
```

### 2) Create a `.env` file
```bash
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
GROQ_API_KEY=gsk_...
```

### 3) Install `python-dotenv`
```bash
pip install python-dotenv
```

### 4) Load the variables in your code
```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()
```

question: How can I use Gemini in the Module 1 notebook with the OpenAI-style endpoint, and what do I need to set up first?
answer_llm:
Use Gemini in the Module 1 notebook by pointing the OpenAI client at Google’s OpenAI-compatible endpoint and using your Gemini model name.

What to set up first:
1. Put your Gemini API key in `.env`, for example `GEMINI_API_KEY`.
2. Load it in the notebook.
3. Create an `OpenAI` client with:
   `base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"`
4. Pass a Gemini model name, such as `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`.

Example:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

def llm(instructions, user_prompt, model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite"):
    message_history = [
        {"role": "developer", "content": instructions},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_prompt}
    ]

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=message_history
    )

    return response.choices[0].message.content
```

Note: this uses the older chat-completions style through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint, so you may need to adjust the notebook in places where it expects the newer Responses API, especially for reading outputs and handling tools/function calls.

question: Why does the Module 1 code need changes when switching from the Responses format to Gemini's chat completions endpoint?
answer_llm:
Because the two APIs return results in different shapes and use different calling patterns.

With the **Responses format**, the notebook code expects things like:

- `response.output`
- `responses.create(...)`
- Responses-style tool/function handling

But Gemini via the **OpenAI-compatible chat completions endpoint** works like:

- `client.chat.completions.create(...)`
- read text from `response.choices[0].message.content`
- adapt tool/function call handling to chat-completions format

So the Module 1 code needs changes mainly in:

1. **How the model is called**
2. **How the answer is read**
3. **How tools/function calls are handled**

In short: the old code is written for the Responses API, but Gemini through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint behaves like chat completions, so the notebook has to be updated to match that interface.

question: How do I get Pydantic structured output from Gemini when the newer responses.parse API isn’t supported on the OpenAI-compatible endpoint?
answer_llm:
Use the **OpenAI SDK’s chat-completions parsing flow** instead of `responses.parse`.

For Gemini on the OpenAI-compatible endpoint:

1. Define your Pydantic schema.
2. Call `client.chat.completions.parse(...)`.
3. Read the parsed object from `response.choices[0].message.parsed`.

Example:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Question(BaseModel):
    question: str
    answer: str

class Questions(BaseModel):
    questions: list[Question]
```

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

messages = [
    {"role": "developer", "content": "Generate three FAQ questions about RAG."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Topic: structured output with Gemini"}
]

response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

This gives you a proper Pydantic object directly, without manual JSON parsing.

2. Call `chat.completions.parse(..., response_format=YourModel)`.

question: How do I install Ollama on macOS, Windows, or Linux for the RAG module?
answer_llm:
To install Ollama for the RAG module:

- **macOS**: go to [https://ollama.com/download](https://ollama.com/download), download the **`.pkg`** file, and install it.
- **Windows**: go to [https://ollama.com/download](https://ollama.com/download), download the **`.msi`** file, and install it.
- **Linux**: run this in the terminal:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
```

After installation, test it with:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```

To verify the local server is running:

```bash
curl http://localhost:11434
```

If needed, install the Python client too:

```bash
pip install ollama
```

question: Why does tiktoken show fewer tokens than the number OpenAI returns for the same chat prompt?
answer_llm:
`tiktoken` is only counting the visible text you pass in, while OpenAI’s chat API adds some hidden formatting tokens for each message and for the assistant reply start.

In particular:

- each chat message adds about 4 structural tokens
- the API adds 2 extra tokens globally
- extra whitespace, newlines, or unusual Unicode can add a bit more

So `tiktoken` may show fewer tokens than OpenAI reports for the same prompt.
answer_orig:
When using `tiktoken.encode()` to count tokens in your prompt, you might see a difference compared to OpenAI’s API response. For instance, you might get 320 tokens, while OpenAI reports 327. This is due to internal tokens added by OpenAI’s chat formatting.

Here’s what happens:

- Each message in a `chat.completions.create()` call (e.g., `{role: "user", content: "..."}`) adds 4 structural tokens (role, content, separators).
- The API adds 2 tokens globally to mark the start of assistant response generation.
- Extra newlines, whitespace, or uncommon Unicode characters in your content may slightly increase the token count.

Thus, even if your visible text is 320 tokens, OpenAI may count 327 due to these internal additions.

question: When I count tokens in my messages locally, why is the API total a bit higher than expected?
answer_llm:
When you count tokens locally, the API can be a bit higher because OpenAI adds internal chat-formatting tokens that aren’t visible in your message text.

In particular:
- Each chat message adds about **4 structural tokens** for role/content/separators.
- The API adds **2 global tokens** to indicate the assistant response start.
- Extra whitespace, newlines, or unusual Unicode can add a few more tokens.

So a local count like 320 can become 327 in the API.

question: How can I stop re-exporting my API key every time I open a new terminal in this RAG project?
answer_llm:
Use `dirdotenv`.

Quick setup:

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv
```

Then add this to your `~/.bashrc` or `~/.zshrc`:

```bash
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh
```

Inside your project, create a `.env` file:

```bash
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the API key is loaded automatically whenever you `cd` into the project directory.

If you already use `direnv`, that also works: put your exports in `.envrc` and run `direnv allow`.
answer_orig:
Use [`dirdotenv`](https://github.com/alexeygrigorev/dirdotenv). It is like `direnv`, but works with both `.env` and `.envrc`, and is more portable across shells and operating systems.

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv

# add this line to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh

# inside your project:
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the key is loaded automatically when you `cd` into the project directory.

question: What's the best way to decide field weights in minsearch or a similar search engine?
answer_llm:
The best way is to **evaluate different field weights against a ground-truth dataset**.

A practical approach:

1. Create a small set of representative questions.
2. Mark which documents should be retrieved for each question.
3. Try different field weight settings.
4. Compare retrieval metrics like **hit rate, precision@k, recall@k, or MRR**.

For small projects, you can also tune weights by trial and error, but **evaluation is the more reliable method**.

question: What should I do to clean up documents before using them for RAG so retrieval works better?
answer_llm:
Clean the documents so they are **clean, structured, and easy to chunk and retrieve**.

Common things to do:
- Remove noise like broken HTML, duplicate text, boilerplate, OCR errors, repeated headers, and repeated footers.
- Preserve useful context such as titles, section names, dates, page numbers, speaker names, and Q&A structure.
- Store the cleaned data in a structured format that’s easy to process, such as JSON.
- Chunk the documents so related context stays together.
- Keep metadata for later filtering or ranking.

The goal is not just to shorten the text, but to make retrieval more accurate.
answer_orig:

- Preserve useful context such as titles, section names, dates, page numbers, speaker names, and Q&A structure.
- Store the result in a structured format that is easy to process. JSON is often convenient, but it is not mandatory.
- Chunk the documents in a way that keeps related context together.
- Keep metadata that may help filtering or ranking later.

The exact format depends on the source data. The goal is not just to make the text shorter, but to make retrieval more accurate.

question: In GitHub Codespaces, I keep seeing "Failed to hardlink files" from uv — does that mean the install broke, or is it safe to ignore?
answer_llm:
No — it’s safe to ignore. In GitHub Codespaces, `uv` may be unable to hardlink files between its cache and the target environment, so it falls back to copying them. The package still installs successfully.

If you want to suppress the warning in the current shell:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make it persistent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```
answer_orig:
No. This warning can happen in GitHub Codespaces when `uv` cannot hardlink files between the cache and the target environment.

The package still installs. `uv` falls back to copying files.

To suppress the warning for the current shell:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make it persistent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

See the `uv` documentation for more details: [https://docs.astral.sh/uv/](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/).

question: Why does WSL2 say my model needs more memory than available when my PC has enough RAM?
answer_llm:
Your WSL2 only has access to the amount of RAM allocated to WSL, not all the RAM in your PC. So even if your computer has enough memory overall, WSL2 may be limited to a smaller amount like `Y.Y GiB`, which causes the model memory error.

To fix it, increase WSL2’s memory limit by creating a `.wslconfig` file in:

`C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`

with something like:

```ini
[wsl2]
memory=8GB
```

Then restart WSL:

```bash
wsl --shutdown
```

After that, check the available memory in WSL with `free`.
answer_orig:
Your WSL2 is set to use Y.Y GiB, not all your computer memory. To allocate more RAM, follow these steps:

1. Create a `.wslconfig` file under your Windows user profile directory: `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`.

2. Include the desired RAM allocation in the file:

   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL using the command:

   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Run the `free` command in WSL to verify the changes.

For more details, read [this article](https://www.aleksandrhovhannisyan.com/blog/limiting-memory-usage-in-wsl-2/).

question: My GitHub Codespace is stuck on "Finishing up" and never connects — what should I try first?
answer_llm:
First, go to [github.com/codespaces](https://github.com/codespaces), **stop the codespace, then start it again**.

If it still gets stuck on “Finishing up,” try opening it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or use a different browser like **Edge, Chrome, or Brave**. As a last resort, **delete the codespace and create a new one**.
answer_orig:
These are usually GitHub Codespaces reliability issues rather than a problem with the course, so there's no single guaranteed fix — but the following workarounds resolve most cases.

**Codespace won't connect / stuck on "Finishing up":**

- Go to [github.com/codespaces](https://github.com/codespaces), stop the codespace, and start it again.
- If it still won't connect, open it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or try a different browser (Edge/Chrome/Brave).
- As a last resort, delete the codespace and create a new one.

**"My setup is all gone":**

- The repo in `/workspaces` persists across stop/start, but a **rebuild or a brand-new codespace** starts from a clean image, and system/global installs outside your project don't always survive. Reinstalling is quick with `uv` (`uv sync` / `uv add ...`).
- **Commit and push your work often** — uncommitted changes survive a stop/start but are lost if you delete or recreate the codespace.

If Codespaces keeps being flaky for you, consider running the course locally instead — see "[Can I run the course locally instead of Codespaces?](026_aa310de435_can-i-run-the-course-locally-instead-of-codespaces.md)".

question: Can I use something other than OpenAI for the RAG assignments, or is the course locked to it?
answer_llm:
No, the course is **not locked to OpenAI**. For the RAG assignments, you can use **any LLM provider** you prefer, as long as it works for your setup.

Examples mentioned in the course context include:

- Other hosted providers like **Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, Mistral**
- Hosted open models via **Hugging Face**
- A **local model** with **Ollama, vLLM, LM Studio**, etc.

If the provider exposes an **OpenAI-compatible endpoint**, you usually only need to change:

- `base_url`
- API key
- model name

If you want, I can also help you pick an alternative that works best for your region or budget.
answer_orig:
If the provider used in the course isn't available or is blocked in your region (or you simply prefer another one), you can use any other LLM provider — the course isn't tied to OpenAI. Just switch to something else:

- Hosted, OpenAI-compatible providers — e.g. Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, Mistral. The course code uses the OpenAI client, so you usually only need to change the `base_url`, the API key, and the model name.
- Open models via Hugging Face (e.g. Qwen, Llama) if you prefer hosted open-source models.
- Serve a model locally with [Ollama](https://ollama.com/), [vLLM](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm), LM Studio, or anything else — no external API call at all, so regional blocks don't apply and you don't need a paid key. Most of these also expose an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, so the course code works with only a `base_url` change.
- Rent a GPU machine and serve the model there (e.g. with vLLM) if your own machine can't run the model you want. This gives you a private OpenAI-compatible endpoint to point the course code at — just remember to stop/delete the instance when you're done so you don't keep paying for it.
- A VPN also works if you just need to reach a provider that's blocked at the network level.

Anything with an OpenAI-compatible endpoint (or a locally served model) will work — pick whatever is available and convenient for you.

For a curated list of options, see [Awesome LLMs](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/awesome-llms.md) in the course repo, which collects OpenAI API alternatives and tools for running models locally.

question: How can I stop OpenAI 429 errors when I’m looping over lots of docs in the notebook?
answer_llm:
To stop `429` errors while looping over lots of docs, focus on reducing API calls and staying under the rate limit:

- **Use retries with backoff**:
  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI
  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```

- **Lower concurrency**: if you’re using threads/processes, keep it small, around **2–3 workers**.

- **Cache results**: save embeddings/LLM outputs to disk and reload them when rerunning the notebook so you don’t call the API again for the same inputs.

- **Batch requests**: if possible, send a list of texts in one call instead of one-by-one in a loop.

- **Use a cheaper/free model/provider**: `gpt-4o-mini` is recommended, and Groq’s free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) has generous limits.

If the error is actually `insufficient_quota`, that’s **not** a rate-limit issue — it means your OpenAI account needs billing credits.
answer_orig:
Free-tier and low-spend OpenAI accounts have per-minute and per-day request limits that are easy to blow through when a notebook loops over many documents. Reduce the number of API calls before you worry about handling the error after the fact.

- **Retry with backoff.** The `openai` Python client retries automatically with exponential backoff; raise the limit so transient 429s resolve themselves instead of crashing the run:

  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI

  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```

question: In agentic RAG, what exactly do people mean by a tool or function, and why do they say the terms are basically the same?
answer_llm:
In agentic RAG, a **tool** or **function** means something the model can **call for external help** when it needs it.

Examples:
- searching documents
- querying a database
- calling an API
- running a calculation

They’re basically the same term in this course because they both refer to an external capability the model can invoke. So the naming difference is not important here.

The key idea is:
- In **agentic RAG**, the model can **decide when to call** a tool/function.
- In **basic RAG**, retrieval is usually a **fixed preprocessing step** that happens before the model answers.

So “tool” and “function” are just two common names for the same kind of callable helper.

In the context of this course, tools and functions are closely related terms. Do not worry too much about the naming difference.

A tool/function is something the model can call when it needs external help, such as searching documents, querying a database, calling an API, or running a calculation.

The important idea is that in agentic RAG, the model can decide when to call a tool/function. In basic RAG, retrieval is usually a fixed step that happens before the model answers.

question: How is tool calling different from normal retrieval in basic RAG, where the search step happens before the answer?
answer_llm:
In basic RAG, retrieval is a fixed preprocessing step: the system searches for relevant documents first, then gives those results to the model to answer.

In agentic RAG, tool calling is different because the model can decide by itself when to call a tool/function, such as search, database query, API call, or calculation, during the reasoning process. So the retrieval/search step is not always done upfront; it happens only if the model chooses to use the tool.

question: Why do I get `AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'` when I call OpenAI Responses API on a model from another provider like Mistral or Groq?
answer_llm:
Because `response.output` is part of **OpenAI’s Responses API**, and non-OpenAI providers like **Mistral, Groq, Gemini, etc.** do not implement that API shape.

So if you call a non-OpenAI model through something that expects `responses.create(...)`, you may end up with a plain string or a different response object, and then `response.output` fails with:

`AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'`

### What to do instead
Use the **chat completions** API for those providers and read:

```python
response.choices[0].message.content
```

Example:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

### Important
You may also need to **adapt the tools schema** to whatever format your provider expects.

In short:  
- `response.output` → OpenAI Responses API only  
- `response.choices[0].message.content` → chat-completions-compatible providers
answer_orig:
The new OpenAI Responses API (`client.responses.create(...)`, accessed via `response.output`) is OpenAI-specific. Other providers (Mistral, Groq, Gemini, etc.) don't implement it.

For non-OpenAI providers, use the chat-completions API and read `response.choices[0].message.content`:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,  # may need adapting per provider
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You'll also have to adapt the tools schema to whatever shape your provider expects.

It is okay to use the older `chat.completions` API for homework or projects if your provider supports that interface better than the Responses API.

question: How should I define the weather tool JSON for Azure OpenAI so it stops throwing the missing tools[0].function error?
What does the get_weather_tool object need to look like when I keep getting Error code 400 about a missing function field?
Is there a specific JSON format for function tools in Azure OpenAI to avoid the tools[0].function invalid_request error?
Can you show the correct get_weather_tool structure for a weather function that takes a city name?
My Azure OpenAI request fails with missing required parameter tools[0].function — what tool definition am I supposed to use?
Why do I get a 404 in toyaikit when I try Groq or another non-OpenAI model, and what should I switch to instead?
answer_llm:
You get the `404` because `toyaikit`’s `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` uses OpenAI’s **Responses API** (`responses.create`), and that endpoint is only implemented by OpenAI.

For Groq or other non-OpenAI providers, switch to the **chat completions** classes instead:

- `OpenAIChatCompletionsClient`
- `OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner`

These use `chat.completions.create`, which Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

If you’re using Anthropic, switch to:

- `AnthropicClient`
- `AnthropicMessagesRunner`

So the short answer is: **don’t use the Responses runner/client for non-OpenAI models; use the chat-completions runner/client instead.**
answer_orig:
If you get a `404` (or similar) when using toyaikit with Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, it's because `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` call OpenAI's Responses endpoint (`responses.create`), which only OpenAI implements. Switch to the **chat completions** classes, which use the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

### OpenAI or Groq (chat completions)

```python
import os
from openai import OpenAI
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)   # functions need type hints + an Args: docstring

# OpenAI:
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="gpt-4o-mini", client=OpenAI())

# Groq (same runner, just point the OpenAI client at Groq's base URL):
groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", client=groq_client)

runner = OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

### Anthropic

```python
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import AnthropicClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import AnthropicMessagesRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)

# Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment automatically:
llm_client = AnthropicClient(model="claude-haiku-4-5")
```

question: What’s the difference between runner.loop() and runner.run() in toyaikit, especially if I just want one programmatic turn?
answer_llm:
`runner.loop(prompt=...)` is the one you want for a single programmatic turn. It runs the agent once and returns a result object with things like:

- `.last_message`
- `.all_messages`
- `.tokens`

`runner.run()` is for the interactive Jupyter loop, not for a one-off scripted call.

So if you just want one turn in code, use:

```python
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

question: Why do I get a 402 error from OpenRouter when I call `responses.create()` in homework?
answer_llm:
You likely get the 402 because OpenRouter checks the **maximum possible output size** for `responses.create()`, and if you don’t set a reasonable `max_output_tokens`, it may assume a very large limit and reject the request for a free/low-limit key.

**Fix:** set a lower `max_output_tokens`, for example:

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

If you’re using `rag_helper.py`, add the same parameter in `llm()`.  
For ToyAIKit, use:

```python
llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

Also make sure your `.env` points to OpenRouter and that your key has enough credits/limits.
answer_orig:
OpenRouter can return APIStatusError with code 402 when responses.create() is called without a reasonable max_output_tokens limit. This happens because OpenRouter bills/limits checks against the maximum possible output (which can be very large, around 65536 tokens), so a free or low-limit key can be rejected before the model runs. This is different from a direct OpenAI endpoint (which typically returns 429 for insufficient quota).

Fix

Pass a lower limit in your responses.create() call:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI()  # uses OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENAI_BASE_URL from .env

response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework with rag_helper.py, add the same parameter in the ``llm()`` method.

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework Q6 with ToyAIKit:

```python
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIClient

llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

1024 is enough for homework answers; you can raise it later if needed.

If it still fails

1) Confirm your `.env` points at OpenRouter:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

2) Check your OpenRouter key limit and remaining credits at https://openrouter.ai/settings/keys

3) Prefer a pinned model (for example `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`) instead of `openrouter/free`, which can route to models with different limits.

Note: This behavior is different from OpenAI’s typical 429 handling for insufficient quota. If you still encounter issues after these steps, double-check the model and endpoint configuration to ensure the key is valid and has sufficient credits.

question: In the Module 1 homework, what does it mean to point RAG at the chunk index instead of the original docs?
answer_llm:
It means you should build and search the RAG index on the **chunked documents**, not the original full documents.

So if your data is split into `chunks`, use those for the index:

```python
index.fit(chunks)
```

not:

```python
index.fit(documents)
```

That way, the RAG pipeline retrieves only the relevant chunks and puts just those into the prompt, which reduces the amount of context sent to the model.
answer_orig:
It means you should build and search the index using the chunked documents, not the original full documents.

For example, if your original records are in `documents` and your split records are in `chunks`, fit the search index on `chunks`:

```python
index.fit(chunks)
```

not:

```python
index.fit(documents)
```

Then the RAG pipeline retrieves relevant chunks and puts only those chunks into the prompt. This is what reduces the amount of context sent to the model.

question: When setting up RAG for Module 1, why do we fit the index on split chunks instead of full documents?
answer_llm:
Because RAG should retrieve only the most relevant pieces of information, not entire documents.

In Module 1, you fit the index on the **split chunks** so that:

- retrieval happens at a smaller, more precise unit of text,
- the prompt contains only the relevant chunks,
- less context is sent to the model, which makes RAG more efficient and accurate.

So you should do:

```python
index.fit(chunks)
```

not:

```python
index.fit(documents)
```

If you fit on full documents, the retriever may return much larger text than needed, which increases prompt size and reduces the benefit of chunk-based retrieval.

question: For the Module 1 homework, if my calculated number is a bit off from the choices, should I still pick the nearest one?
answer_llm: For Module 1, if your result is a bit off and it’s a multiple-choice homework, choose the **closest option**.
answer_orig:
Not always. If your numeric answer is close to one of the options, choose the closest option.

- Slightly different filtering.
- Different dataset versions.
- Floating-point or rounding differences.

question: How can I check the token count for Module 1 homework if I'm using a different LLM provider?
answer_llm:
Check the token count from the **model response object**.

If you’re using an OpenAI-compatible client, look for fields like:

- `response.usage.input_tokens`
- `response.usage.prompt_tokens`

If you use a non-OpenAI provider, inspect that provider’s **usage fields** and adapt your code. Don’t use `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, Groq, or other providers, because tokenization differs by model.

If the provider doesn’t expose token usage, use the provider’s **native tokenizer** as an approximation.
answer_orig:
For the current Module 1 homework, get the token count from the model response object.

For example, OpenAI-compatible clients usually return usage information on the response, such as `response.usage.input_tokens` or `response.usage.prompt_tokens`, depending on the API style.

If you use a non-OpenAI provider, check the provider's response object for its usage fields and adapt the code. Do not use `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, Groq, or other providers because tokenization differs by model.

If your provider does not expose token usage, use that provider's native tokenizer as an approximation. For multiple-choice homework questions, choose the closest option.

question: Why does my homework link get rejected with a 500 or other non-200 error, and what should I check first?
answer_llm:
The submission checker sends a GET request to your link and expects an HTTP `200` response. If it gets `500` or another non-200, the link is usually not publicly reachable.

Check these first:

- **Is the repo public?** If it’s private, make it public.
- **Did you include `.git`?** Submit the plain repo URL, not `.../repo.git`.
- **Is there a typo in the URL?** Paste the exact link.

Quick test: open the URL in an incognito/private browser window. If it works there, it should work for the checker too.

question: What exactly are embeddings in vector search, and why do we turn text into numbers?
answer_llm:
Embeddings are numerical representations of text that preserve meaning and context.

Why turn text into numbers?
- Computers can’t directly compare words by meaning, but they can compare numbers.
- If similar texts are converted into similar vectors, then we can use math to measure semantic similarity.
- This makes vector search possible: we compare the query vector with document vectors to find the closest matches.

In short: embeddings let us represent text in a vector space so that “similar meaning” becomes “nearby numbers.”
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: How do embeddings keep meaning and context when converting non-numerical data into numerical values?

In other words, if two pieces of text are semantically related, their embeddings should end up close together in vector space. That proximity lets mathematical similarity methods capture meaning and context.

question: Why did the lesson just use .dot(...) for similarity, but the homework keeps talking about normalization?
answer_llm:
The lesson used `.dot(...)` directly because the embeddings were assumed to already be **normalized to unit length**. For unit vectors, the dot product is the same as **cosine similarity**.

In the homework, the embeddings you may be using are **not normalized by default**, so the dot product is **not** cosine similarity unless you normalize the vectors first.

So the key difference is:

- **Lesson:** normalized embeddings → `.dot(...)` works as cosine similarity
- **Homework:** embeddings may not be normalized → you need to normalize before using dot product
answer_orig:
In the lesson, `.dot(...)` was used under the assumption that the embeddings returned by the model (e.g., `model.encode(...)` from OpenAI) are already normalized to have unit length. In that case, the dot product is mathematically equivalent to cosine similarity.

In the homework, however, we use classic embeddings like TF-IDF + SVD, which are not normalized by default. This means that the dot product does not represent cosine similarity unless we manually normalize the vectors.

- **If embeddings are not normalized**:

question: For vector search, is it better to store embeddings from the question, the answer, or both together?
answer_llm:
There’s no single right answer — it’s something to experiment with on your dataset.

The course suggests trying three options:

- **Embed the answer only**
- **Embed the question only**
- **Embed `question + " " + answer`** — often the best, but longer and slightly more costly

Then compare which gives the best **hit rate / MRR** on your ground-truth set.
answer_orig:
There's no single right answer — it's an experiment to run on your dataset. The course shows three options:

- Embed the answer (`text`) only — works because the model captures semantic similarity between questions and their answers.
- Embed the question only — works because user queries look like the indexed questions.
- Embed `question + " " + text` — often the best, but produces longer input and slightly more cost.

Pick whichever gives the best hit rate / MRR on your ground-truth set. The course materials include a side-by-side comparison.

It won’t always be the best option, but it can work well enough to match user queries to relevant answers.

question: What exactly does cosine similarity measure between two vectors?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity measures **how aligned two vectors are**, specifically the **cosine of the angle between them**.

- If the angle is small, the vectors are very similar.
- If the vectors are perpendicular, the similarity is 0.
- If they point in opposite directions, the similarity is -1.

It ignores magnitude when the vectors are normalized, focusing on direction instead.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity is a measure used to calculate the similarity between two non-zero vectors, often used in text analysis to determine how similar two documents are based on their content. This metric computes the cosine of the angle between two vectors, which are typically word counts or TF-IDF values of the documents. The cosine similarity value ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 indicates that the vectors are identical, 0 indicates that the vectors are orthogonal (no similarity), and -1 represents completely opposite vectors.

question: How do you calculate similarity between documents using cosine similarity in text analysis?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity compares two document vectors by measuring the cosine of the angle between them.

In text analysis, you usually:

1. Convert each document into a vector, such as word counts or TF-IDF values.
2. Compute the dot product between the two vectors.
3. If the vectors are normalized to unit length, that dot product is the cosine similarity.

The value ranges from:
- **1** = identical documents
- **0** = no similarity
- **-1** = opposite vectors

So, for documents, higher cosine similarity means their content is more similar.

question: Why can cosine similarity be computed just with a dot product after normalizing the embeddings?
answer_llm:
Because once the embeddings are **normalized to unit length**, their magnitudes are all 1. Then cosine similarity

\[
\cos(\theta) = \frac{a \cdot b}{\|a\|\|b\|}
\]

simplifies to just

\[
a \cdot b
\]

since \(\|a\| = \|b\| = 1\).

So after normalization, cosine similarity can be computed efficiently with a plain dot product.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are, regardless of their magnitude. When all vectors (including the query) are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes no longer matter. In this case, cosine similarity is equivalent to simply taking the dot product between the query and each document embedding. This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: How does normalizing vectors to unit length make cosine similarity ignore magnitude?
answer_llm:
Normalizing vectors to unit length removes magnitude because each vector is rescaled so its length becomes 1.

After that, cosine similarity depends only on the angle between the vectors:

- the dot product of two unit vectors is exactly their cosine similarity
- any original size differences are divided out during normalization

So two vectors pointing in the same direction but with different lengths become identical for cosine similarity purposes.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are, regardless of their magnitude. When all vectors (including the query) are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes no longer matter. In this case, cosine similarity is equivalent to simply taking the dot product between the query and each document embedding. This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: In vector search, why does comparing the query to all documents turn into matrix multiplication?
answer_llm:
Because once the document embeddings and the query embedding are represented as vectors, comparing the query against every document is just doing the same dot product over and over.

If the vectors are normalized, cosine similarity becomes a dot product. Then all document vectors can be stacked into a matrix, and the query stays a vector. Computing all similarities at once is exactly:

- matrix of document embeddings × query vector

So what would be a loop over documents turns into one efficient matrix multiplication.

question: What is the reason cosine similarity between unit vectors is the same as their dot product?
answer_llm:
Because unit vectors have magnitude 1, the cosine similarity formula

\[
\cos(\theta) = \frac{u \cdot v}{\|u\| \, \|v\|}
\]

simplifies to:

\[
\cos(\theta) = u \cdot v
\]

when both vectors are normalized to unit length. So for unit vectors, cosine similarity is the same as their dot product.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are, regardless of their magnitude. When all vectors (including the query) are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes no longer matter. This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: How does using normalized embeddings make similarity search much faster?
answer_llm:
Using normalized embeddings makes similarity search faster because cosine similarity becomes just a dot product.

When all vectors are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes don’t matter anymore, so you can compute similarity with a matrix multiplication between the query vector and all document embeddings. That is much more efficient than computing cosine similarity separately for each pair.

question: Why does download.py stay stuck at 0% when pulling model.onnx from HuggingFace, and how can I fix it?
answer_llm:
This happens because the connection to HuggingFace’s CDN is slow or blocked, so `download.py` appears to sit at 0%.

Fix it by downloading the file manually in your browser:

https://huggingface.co/Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2/resolve/main/onnx/model.onnx

Save it to:

```bash
models/Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2/model.onnx
```

The script checks whether the file already exists locally before downloading, so once it’s there, it will skip the download and continue normally.

question: Why do the matrix and loop versions of vector search not give exactly the same scores?
answer_llm:
Because of floating-point precision and the order of addition.

`X.dot(v_query)` uses optimized BLAS code, while the loop adds the same numbers sequentially in Python. Since floating-point addition is not perfectly associative, the two methods can differ in the last few decimal places even though they compute the same dot products.

So the scores are effectively the same, just not exactly identical. Use a tolerance check like:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```
answer_orig:
In [Lesson 4 (Vector Search)](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/02-vector-search/lessons/04-vector-search.md) we compute the scores two ways — `scores = X.dot(v_query)` and the equivalent for-loop — and they can come out slightly different. This is normal, it's just floating-point precision. If you compare them with `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` you might get `False`, even though both compute the same dot products. They just add the numbers up in a slightly different order, and floating-point addition isn't perfectly associative, so the results can differ in the last few decimal places.

Under the hood, `X.dot(v)` runs optimized BLAS code that sums in a different order than a sequential Python loop. The math is the same; only the rounding differs, so the values aren't really "wrong."

Just compare them with a small tolerance instead of expecting an exact match:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

question: Why am I getting an IndexError: list index out of range when I try to access the best chunk in my vector search code?
answer_llm:
The most likely reason is that the number of embeddings does not match the number of chunks.

Make sure you generate embeddings directly from the chunk list, like this:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

Then `X` should have the same number of rows as `len(chunks)`. If they don’t match, indexing into the “best chunk” result can produce `IndexError: list index out of range`.
answer_orig:

```python

X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

The number of rows in `X` should be equal to `len(chunks)`.

question: In module 2, why does accessing the top chunk fail if my embedding matrix size is different from the number of chunks?
answer_llm:
Accessing the top chunk fails because the number of embeddings must match the number of chunks. If your embedding matrix has more or fewer rows than the chunk list, the index you pick for the “best chunk” can point past the end of the chunks array, causing an `IndexError`.

Make sure you generate embeddings directly from the chunk contents, like:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

Then `X.shape[0]` should equal `len(chunks)`. If they don’t match, the top similarity index won’t correspond correctly to a chunk.

question: Why is my Q2 cosine similarity from Module 2 homework not matching any answer choice?
answer_llm:
The most likely reason is that you’re using a different embedding model than the homework expects.

For Module 2 homework, you should use:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

not `sentence-transformers` or a lesson model like `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` / `all-mpnet-base-v2`.

Also check that you are:

- embedding the page’s `content` field,
- comparing it to the query vector from Q1,
- using the fact that the `Embedder` already returns normalized vectors, so the dot product is the cosine similarity.

If it’s still slightly off, pick the closest option.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is using a different embedding model than the homework specifies. Homework 2 tells you **not** to use `sentence-transformers` and to use the lightweight ONNX `Embedder` from `embedder.py` instead:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

Both approaches produce the same vectors for the same model, but if you embed with a different model (for example the `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` model from the lessons, or `all-mpnet-base-v2`) you'll get a cosine value that isn't among the options.

Also check that:

- You're embedding the page's `content` field (not the filename or the whole dict).
- You're comparing against the query vector from Q1.
- The vectors are normalized — the `Embedder` returns normalized vectors, so the dot product is the cosine similarity directly.

If your value is still slightly off after using the right model, pick the closest option — small numerical differences are expected.

question: How do I set up the Gemini key for Kestra so the flows can actually read it?
answer_llm:
Set both variables **before starting Kestra**, then restart it:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)

docker compose up -d
```

Notes:
- `GEMINI_API_KEY` is the **plain** key.
- `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` is the **base64-encoded** one that flows read.
- In flows, reference it as:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

If it still doesn’t work, make sure:
- you started Kestra from the same shell where you exported the vars,
- the base64 value wasn’t corrupted by a newline,
- and you restarted the container after setting the env vars.
answer_orig:
Kestra reads secrets from environment variables that are prefixed with `SECRET_` and whose value is **base64-encoded**. You export them in the terminal *before* starting Kestra. For Gemini you need two variables — the plain one (used by the AI Copilot) and the base64-encoded `SECRET_` one (used by the flows):

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"                              # used by AI Copilot
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)             # used by the flows
export SECRET_OPENAI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-openai-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for flow 3
export SECRET_TAVILY_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-tavily-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for web search (flows 3, 5, 6)
```

Then start (or restart) Kestra so it picks up the variables:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

Inside a flow, reference a secret **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

See the [setup lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/03-setup.md) for the full walkthrough. Never commit your keys to Git.

question: Do I need both a normal Gemini API key and a SECRET_ version, or is one enough?
answer_llm:
One is enough for the **flows**, but in Kestra you generally need **both**:

question: Why does Kestra AI Copilot keep saying it can only help with creating Kestra flows, and how can I make it work with Gemini?
answer_llm:
Kestra AI Copilot says that because it didn’t receive a valid Gemini API key, so it falls back to a default refusal message.

To make it work with Gemini in Kestra Open Source:

1. Set the **plain** environment variable `GEMINI_API_KEY`
2. Restart Kestra

Example:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

Important:
- The Copilot uses **`GEMINI_API_KEY`**
- The flows use **`SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`**
- They are different, so export both if you need both features

If it still doesn’t work:
- Check the variable is really set in the same shell:
  ```bash
  echo $GEMINI_API_KEY
  ```
- Generate a fresh key in Google AI Studio
- You may have hit the free-tier limit (`429 Resource Exhausted`), so wait and try again

If you want, I can also show you how to set both keys correctly in `docker-compose`.
answer_orig:
This message means the AI Copilot didn't get a valid Gemini API key, so it falls back to a canned refusal. In Kestra's Open Source edition the Copilot only supports Gemini, and it reads the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` variable (not the base64-encoded `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` that the flows use).

Make sure you exported the plain key before starting Kestra, then restart it:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

If it still fails, the key is usually missing, mistyped, or rate-limited:

- Confirm the variable is actually set in the shell you ran `docker compose up` from (`echo $GEMINI_API_KEY`).
- Generate a fresh key in [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com/app/apikey).
- If you've been running the agent/multi-agent flows a lot, you may have hit the free-tier quota (`429 Resource Exhausted`) — wait a minute and retry.

Note that the Copilot needs the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` while the flows need `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` — export both.


Then restart Kestra.

question: How can I tell if the Gemini API key is missing, wrong, or I’ve just hit the quota limit in Kestra?
answer_llm:
In Kestra, the usual clue is the error/message you get:

- **Missing or wrong plain `GEMINI_API_KEY`**: the AI Copilot falls back to  
  **“I can only assist with creating Kestra flows”** because it didn’t get a valid key.
- **Wrong/invalid encoded secret for flows**: flow auth fails because the base64-decoded secret is incorrect.
- **Quota/rate limit hit**: you may see **`429 Resource Exhausted`**. In that case, you’ve likely hit the free-tier limit — wait a minute and retry.

Quick checks:
- `echo $GEMINI_API_KEY` in the same shell where you ran `docker compose up`
- make sure you exported **both**:
  - `GEMINI_API_KEY` for the Copilot
  - `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` for flows
- if you get `429 Resource Exhausted`, it’s quota/rate limiting rather than a missing key

If you want, I can also show a small checklist to distinguish the three cases fast.

question: Why does my Kestra flow say the Gemini key is invalid even though I already set SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY?
answer_llm:
Most likely, your `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` got encoded incorrectly.

The common issue is a trailing newline or space when base64-encoding the key, which makes the decoded secret invalid. Use `echo -n` and quote the variable:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Also check these:

- Restart Kestra after exporting the variable so it picks up the new env var:
  ```bash
  docker compose up -d
  ```
- In your flow, reference it as:
  ```yaml
  {{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
  ```
  not `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`.
- Make sure you exported it in the same shell session you used to start Kestra.

I don't know if the issue is something else.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is a corrupted base64 value. If you encode the key with a trailing space or newline, the decoded secret is wrong and authentication fails.

Use `echo -n` (which omits the trailing newline) and quote the value:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Other things to check:

- You must **restart** Kestra after exporting, so the container picks up the new variables: `docker compose up -d` (or `docker compose down && docker compose up -d`).
- Reference the secret in flows **without** the `SECRET_` prefix: `{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}`.
- Export the variables in the same shell session you start Kestra from.

A cleaner alternative is to put the keys in a `.env` file and pass it explicitly:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```

question: Why should I use an orchestrator like Kestra instead of just running everything in a notebook?
answer_llm:
Because a notebook is great for **experimenting**, but not for running a **reliable production workflow**.

Kestra adds things notebooks usually don’t handle well, like:

- **Scheduling**: run jobs automatically on a timer or event
- **Retries**: recover from failures without manual intervention
- **Monitoring / observability**: see what ran, what failed, and why
- **Secret management**: handle API keys and credentials safely
- **Task chaining**: reliably connect multiple steps together

So the idea is: use notebooks to explore, but use an orchestrator like Kestra when you need a workflow that runs repeatedly and reliably.
answer_orig:
Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but real AI workflows need more than a script that runs once: scheduling, retries, monitoring, secret management, and reliably chaining tasks together. That's what an orchestrator like Kestra provides.

In this module Kestra is also the vehicle for the AI techniques the course is teaching: AI Copilot to generate flows from natural language, RAG to ground responses in real data, and AI agents that decide which tools to call. The goal is to see how AI fits into production-style workflows, not just notebook cells.

Kestra's AI plugins also work with any major provider (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, and more), so you can swap providers in a flow without changing anything else. See the [module intro](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/01-intro.md) for the full motivation.

question: If a notebook can run the code, what extra stuff does Kestra actually give me for a real AI workflow?

Kestra gives you the “real workflow” parts that a notebook doesn’t:

- scheduling
- retries
- monitoring / observability
- secret management
- reliable task chaining
- separation of business logic from orchestration logic

So instead of “a notebook that runs once,” you get a production-style workflow that can run on a schedule, recover from failures, and be managed centrally. Kestra is also used in the course to demonstrate AI Copilot, RAG, and AI agents in those workflows.

question: Do I really need orchestration for AI projects, or is it only useful when jobs need scheduling and retries?
answer_llm:
Orchestration is useful for much more than just scheduling and retries.

In the course context, the main reasons are:

- **Scheduling**: run jobs automatically at the right time
- **Retries**: recover from failures reliably
- **Monitoring**: see what ran, what failed, and why
- **Secret management**: keep credentials organized
- **Chaining tasks**: connect multiple steps into one workflow

So for AI projects, orchestration is valuable when you want **production-style workflows**, not just a notebook or a one-off script.

That said, you **don’t always need it**. For the capstone project, you can even use a plain Python script, and a notebook is acceptable with fewer points for the ingestion-pipeline criterion.

So the short answer is: **No, it’s not only for scheduling and retries, but it’s also not mandatory for every project.**

question: Does Kestra work only with Gemini, or can I swap in Groq, Ollama, or a local model too?
answer_llm:
Kestra is **not limited to Gemini**. You can swap in **Groq, Ollama, or a local model** too, as long as the provider is OpenAI-compatible or supported by Kestra’s AI plugin.

In short:
- **Gemini** is just the course default.
- Kestra supports **OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, xAI/Grok**, and **any OpenAI-compatible provider**.
- You can use **local models via Ollama or LM Studio**.
- For providers like **Groq**, you can point Kestra’s OpenAI plugin at the provider’s base URL.

So yes, you can change the provider block in your flow without changing the rest of the workflow.
answer_orig:
Kestra's AI plugin is provider-agnostic: it supports OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, xAI, Grok, and any OpenAI-compatible provider, including local models served through Ollama or LM Studio. You swap the provider block in a flow without changing anything else. See the [full list of supported providers](https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-ai/provider).

The course uses Gemini because it has a generous free tier, but you are free to use any provider. The [awesome-llms list](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/awesome-llms.md) in the course repo tracks free and free-tier options; Groq is a popular choice because it is OpenAI-compatible and works with both the chat completions and responses APIs.

There are two ways to use AI in Kestra:

- The **AI plugin** (`io.kestra.plugin.ai`) is the generic one. It is the most flexible for switching providers, though new vendor-specific API features take a bit longer to land here.
- The **provider-specific plugins** (e.g. `plugin-gemini`, `plugin-openai`) expose features unique to that vendor, such as Gemini video generation, before they reach the generic AI plugin.

For OpenAI-compatible providers that don't have their own plugin (DeepSeek, Groq, xAI), point the OpenAI plugin at the provider's base URL instead of the default endpoint.

question: Is there a way to build Kestra flows without editing YAML at all, like some kind of no-code setup?
answer_llm:
Yes. Kestra has no-code options, so you don’t have to edit YAML by hand:

- **No-code form editor**: build a flow from a form instead of writing YAML.
- **AI Copilot**: describe what you want in natural language, and it generates the flow for you.
- **External agent skills**: tools like Claude Code, Codex, or Kestra CTL can generate and push flows from your editor.

So while YAML is the underlying format, you can create flows without manually editing it.
answer_orig:
Yes. You have three options that require no hand-written YAML or code:

- **The no-code form editor** builds a flow from a form instead of YAML. It is form-based (not drag-and-drop like n8n), so it suits people who don't want to edit YAML directly.
- **The AI Copilot** generates a flow from a natural-language description, and you can iterate with follow-up messages to add tasks or change the order. See the [AI Copilot lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/04-ai-copilot.md).
- **External agent skills** (Claude Code, Codex, Kestra CTL) let you generate flows and push them into Kestra from your editor without touching the Kestra UI.

Unlike Airflow, where business logic and orchestration logic are intertwined in Python, Kestra keeps the two separate: your Python script stays a plain script, and the workflow YAML just describes how and when to run it. This is closer to a cron job than to a traditional Airflow DAG.

For simple point-to-point automations (e.g. "post a Slack message when a YouTube video goes live"), a tool like Zapier or n8n may be simpler and cheaper. Kestra's advantage is observability, retries, and handling more complex or technical workflows in one place.

question: Why does ChatGPT invent Kestra YAML fields like bucket and name instead of the real ones?
answer_llm:
Because ChatGPT is not grounded in the Kestra plugin schema for your exact Kestra version, it can guess plausible-sounding fields that are actually invalid.

For example, it may invent:

- `bucket` and `name` for GCS upload
- `projectId` / `dataset` / `table` for BigQuery load

when the real fields are different, such as:

- `to` for `io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload`
- `destinationTable` for `io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs`

So the short answer is: it’s hallucinating field names based on pattern matching, not reading the real Kestra docs.

To avoid this:

- Check the official Kestra plugin docs for your version
- Validate the YAML in your Kestra environment
- Use Kestra’s built-in AI Copilot, which is grounded in the current schema

If you want, I can also help verify a specific Kestra YAML snippet.
answer_orig:
A general AI (e.g., ChatGPT) isn't grounded in Kestra's plugin schemas for your running version, so it can surface plausible but invalid property names (such as `bucket`/`name`) instead of the real, version-specific property. For example:

**GCS Upload** — invented `bucket`/`name` props vs. the real `to` property:

```yaml
# Incorrect (invented props)
- id: upload_to_gcs
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
  bucket: "my-bucket"
  name: "path/to/file"

# Correct
- id: upload_to_gcs
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
  to: "gs://my-bucket/path/to/file"
```

**BigQuery LoadFromGcs** — split `projectId`/`dataset`/`table` vs. the real `destinationTable`:

```yaml
# Incorrect (pseudo-split properties)
- id: load_to_bq
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  projectId: "my-project"
  dataset: "my_dataset"
  table: "my_table"

# Correct
- id: load_to_bq
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  destinationTable: "my-project.my_dataset.my_table"
```

To avoid this:

- Cross-check generated YAML against the official plugin docs: https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-gcp
- Use Kestra's built-in AI Copilot, which is grounded in the current plugin schema for your running version.
- Validate and test your YAML in your Kestra environment to ensure it parses and runs as expected.

Note that plugin properties can vary by plugin version; what's correct in one release may be invalid in another. If you share a snippet, we can help verify it against the docs.

question: Why do we bother tracking token usage in Kestra flows, is it mainly about keeping LLM costs under control?
answer_llm:
Yes — mainly for **cost control**, but not only that.

In Kestra flows, token usage is tracked because LLM providers bill by tokens, so knowing usage helps you:

- **Control costs** and catch expensive runs early
- **Optimize prompts** by reducing unnecessary input/output tokens
- **Set guardrails** in scheduled or looping flows so a runaway task doesn’t create a big bill

So it’s about keeping LLM spend under control, but also about making prompts more efficient and adding safety limits.
answer_orig:
Token usage is tracked because it maps directly to what LLM providers charge you for, and Kestra workflows run LLM calls on a schedule or in loops where costs add up fast. The main reasons:

- **Cost control**: tokens are the unit providers bill by, so tracking usage lets you budget, forecast, and spot cost spikes before they get expensive.
- **Prompt optimization**: seeing how many tokens each prompt and response consumes helps you tighten prompts and trim output without losing quality.
- **Guardrails**: in long-running or looping flows, usage monitoring lets you set thresholds and alerts so a runaway task doesn't quietly rack up a large bill.

In practice, each AI task in a Kestra flow can capture usage from the provider's response (for example `usage.total_tokens`), and you can surface those numbers in logs, metrics, or dashboards to keep an eye on spend per run.

question: How does checking tokens help when you're tuning prompts or trying to make outputs shorter?
answer_llm: Checking tokens helps you see how much of your prompt is actually being used, which is useful when tuning prompts and trying to shorten outputs. It lets you spot where hidden token overhead comes from, like chat formatting, extra newlines, or whitespace, and adjust your prompt so it’s more efficient.

question: Why does Docker Desktop say "mounts denied" when I try to run Kestra, and what path do I need to share?
answer_llm:
Docker Desktop shows **“mounts denied”** because it’s not allowed to access the host folder that Kestra is trying to mount. For Kestra, this is commonly the **`/tmp/kestra-wd`** directory.

You need to share:

- **`/tmp`**  
  or, more specifically,
- **`/tmp/kestra-wd`**

In Docker Desktop:

1. Open **Docker Desktop**
2. Go to **Settings**
3. **Resources > File Sharing**
4. Add **`/tmp`** or **`/tmp/kestra-wd`**
5. Click **Apply & restart**

After that, rerun `docker compose up -d`.
answer_orig:
This error means Docker Desktop isn't allowed to access the host path the container is trying to mount (commonly `/tmp/kestra-wd` when running Kestra). Add the path to Docker Desktop's file sharing list:

1. Open Docker Desktop.
2. Click the gear icon (Settings) in the top right.
3. Go to **Resources > File Sharing**.
4. Click the **+** (Add) button.
5. Enter `/tmp` (or the specific path like `/tmp/kestra-wd`) and press Enter.
6. Click **Apply & restart**.

Once Docker restarts, rerun `docker compose up -d` to start Kestra.

question: For the Module 3 Kestra homework, what am I supposed to put in the public repo if almost everything was built in the Kestra UI and not in a normal code editor?
answer_llm:
Put the **Kestra flow YAML files** you created or edited into the public repo.

Since most of the work was done in the Kestra UI, the repo should contain:

- the **exported/downloaded flow YAMLs**
- any **scripts** those flows call, if you changed any
- optionally a **README** summarizing what you did

A good structure would be something like:

- `module-3/flows/`
  - `flow1.yaml`
  - `flow2.yaml`

Then make the repo public and submit that link. The **form answers themselves** go into the homework submission fields separately.

question: Why am I getting `JSONDecodeError: Expecting value` when I try to parse the LLM output with `json.loads`?
answer_llm:
The LLM is likely returning something that is not raw JSON — for example, it may wrap the JSON in a Markdown code fence or add extra prose before/after it. In that case, `json.loads(response)` fails with:

```python
JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)
```

To fix it, force the model to output JSON only:

```python
response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    response_format={"type": "json_object"},
)
parsed = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)
```

Also make the prompt very explicit about the output shape, for example:

```text
Output a JSON object with a single key "questions" whose value is a list of 5 strings.
Do not include any extra text, explanation, or formatting.
```

If you want, I can also show you how to safely strip code fences before parsing.

question: Why does my Jupyter kernel keep crashing when I try to embed the ground-truth data in the evaluation notebook?
answer_llm:
Your Jupyter kernel is likely crashing because the machine runs out of RAM. When an embedding model is loaded together with the rest of the notebook state, small-RAM environments like default Codespaces or low-end laptops can exceed memory.

Workarounds:
- Use a smaller embedder, e.g. `sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2` (384-dim).
- Move the embedding step into a separate Python script, save the vectors, and load them back into the notebook.
- Use a Codespaces machine type with more RAM or run locally.
- Embed the ground-truth set in batches and free memory between batches with `del` and `gc.collect()`.
answer_orig:
Small-RAM machines (Codespaces default, low-end laptops) run out of memory when an embedding model is loaded alongside the rest of the notebook state.

Workarounds:

- Switch to a smaller embedder. `sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2` (384-dim) is a common drop-in. Note: switching models will change your hit-rate / MRR numbers, so re-run the eval after the switch.
- Move the embedding step into a separate Python script that you run from the terminal, then load the saved vectors back into the notebook.
- Use a Codespaces machine type with more RAM (Settings → "Machine type" on a Codespace), or run locally.
- Process the ground-truth set in batches and free memory between batches (`del`, `gc.collect()`).

question: What’s the best way to embed the ground-truth set in batches and release memory between them to stop crashes?
answer_llm:
Process the ground-truth set in batches, and after each batch explicitly free the references and trigger garbage collection:

- `del` the batch embeddings / temporary variables
- `gc.collect()`

A common pattern is:
1. Embed one batch
2. Save or append the vectors
3. Delete the batch objects
4. Call `gc.collect()`
5. Move to the next batch

If crashes still happen, the context also suggests using a smaller embedder or moving the embedding step into a separate Python script.
